"Wise and kindly", "Very pale is the face of Kah-sha as he speaks, and his voice is full of sadness.", "Kah-sha's eager face assents; he will not delay the reading by so much as a word."

At one moment in the book, Kah‑sha attempts to heal a wounded child while villagers observe. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Kah-sha might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: I would make his blood run, Ka-kee. I can do it. See my hand!
My little ones, you bring with you none of the sorrow of your mothers; Well, it were sadder if you did; this world will be darker yet when there are no sunbeams.
Get in, Usha, and take the paddle. I can spring in when it is off the sands.
Listen, child, story of the river will heal your pain; let words flow softly.
passage:
And drawing off his upper garment over his head, he takes from a curious, bead-wrought bag, worn over his breast, a rattle—the insignia of his power! With even more frantic and horrible gyrations than those he had used over the dying chief, but occupying much less time, his incantation is ended and the swooncollapse has succeeded. Where all has been tempest and uproar the silence of the dead now reigns, but it is a silence tense with expectancy. The spirit of the sorcerer returns. Amid the shiverings of his body the voice comes in those far-away, sepulchral tones which always inspire the people with awe and terror. "I have found the demon-there!" And springing to his feet, his long, bony finger is thrust out toward a middle-aged woman near the door. His body bends rigidly toward her; his eyes seem to cauterize her every nerve of motion; a deathly pallor overspreads her face; her eyes are distended in horror; but she neither moves nor speaks. "Take her!" he cries. "That demon will not burnonly drowning can destroy her power." It is not now a difficult matter to make way through the crowd. Strong men have seized the woman and drag her out of the doorway. The tide is far out. A heavy stake is soon driven securely into the hard beach, a little more than half-way out, where the returning tide, wave by wave, will rise far above the head of the victim. The unfortunate witch has been found harboring the evil spirit that caused the disaster. And here she is lashed to her stake as Shahehe was to hers. Having made the witch thus secure to her fate, the slow, sure coming of which will prove her torture, the excitement of the outsiders abates to such an extent that, weary as all the feasters are, they gladly seek refuge from the storm within the sheltering homes of the village. As one dead still lies little Ch-one, save that now and then a short sigh escapes his white lips. Grimly his father sits watching the child, while the mother, sobbing, crouches over the precious body. Now she puts back his damp hair and covers his brow with kisses, calling over all the pet names she ever gave him. Tashekah in tender sympathy drops silent tears over the sleeping babe in her lap, and dozes from utter exhaustion. Wise and kindly Kah-sha remains, and a few other friends; while the multitude have gone to their homes or
KIN-DA-SHON'S WIFE: 

Already rolled themselves up in their blankets here. the air is resonant with the signs of heavy sleep. The little horn dishes of tallow with their twisted wicks have long ago burned out, but Kah-sha has found material to replenish one, and it burns near the sorrowing group. "I would make his blood run, Ka-kee," Kah-sha says, while fingering gently but intelligently the child's head. "That was a heavy blow. The head bone pushes in. The blood is thick and will spoil in there-better set it moving." "See, Kah-sha," Ka-kee answers in an unusual voice, extending his weak and trembling hand. "Is that ready to do such work? If the wind falls before morning he will live." "I can do it," says Kah-sha, not noticing the last remark. "See my hand!" And as he speaks he holds out his firm and well-shaped hand, with a small, sharp knife in its grasp. "Do it, then, if you like," groans the father; and with a skilful turn of the instrument the deed is done. A little gentle friction over the surface, a chafing of the hands and feet, and, drop by drop, the slow blood begins to flow from the newly opened wound. A little more, and a small stream of the bright fluid is discharged. Circulation is further stimulated, and wet cloths are bound about the child's head. His breathing grows more natural, and the color begins to return to the lips. Sleep overpowers the weary watchers, heavy with sorrow. The tallow lamp burns out. The village sleeps.

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a novel with ethnographic elements published in 1892 by Carrie M. W. Willard, a American writer born in 1853.
main_question:
The character in question here is Sa-allie, who is described this way in the book:
"Sa-allie's face is not at all like her husband's; it is round and rosy, though its roses are hidden just now by the black paint; it is fat and laughing, except just while the crying lasts. In her capacious lap she holds two nursing children, one not yet two years old, the other about four.", "the look of consternation in Sa-allie's face."

At one moment in the book, After rescuing a boy, Sa-allie explains his condition to Kin‑da‑shon and the villagers. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Sa-allie might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: He has never stood on them since that dreadful night. All his life is in his head. You can see that he knows things that other people never think.
To take another wife has been long in your heart, my husband. Why? So much you have talked about this thing I'd like to know what you want a wife for.
Kotch-kul-ah, you are a good wife, keep the child safe.
He is ill, Kin‑da‑shon, his breath thin, his skin pale as moonlight, and his eyes hold the fire of the village's sorrow. We must heal him before night now.
passage:
With the directness of an arrow Kin-da-shon has shot under the breaker, and with swift, strong strokes, timed to the waves, soon reaches the child, whose hands seem frozen to the log's rough bark-scarcely conscious of either his danger or his rescue. His hands are loosened and his sturdy little body is thrown astride the log; then, holding and pushing before him his buoyant burden, Kin-da-shon seeks a landing further down among the mud flats, and succeeds in getting ashore with few bruises either to himself or the boy. It is Kunz; a good deal shaken up, but he soon gets upon his feet and shares the anxiety of the now aroused village in regard to the fate of his companion. It was 

Chan-ka, nephew of Yealh-neddy, that was with him; and Yealh-neddy at this moment is lying within his wife's house, heavy with drunken sleep, a result of last night's feast, for which he had provided several boxes of hoots-anoo of his own manufacture. Now, after several hours of wild excitement, he and many others are lying in disgusting stupefaction. Already among those who have gathered on the beach. KIN-DA-SHON'S WIFE: 

is heard the sound of wailing for the dead; but it is not such as is made for those who die on shore. There is no death so held in terror by the Kling-gets as that by drowning-doomed as they believe such a soul is to eternal wandering in cold and pain, ever and vainly seeking rest. The shrieks and the groans of the women are heard high above the thundering of the waters, which are dashing and breaking on the shore. Some of the men have gone to look at the boat. As they turn over its ruined, broken hull they find the sailsticks wedged into it and the sail curiously wrapped and caught between them. Stop! There is something within. Can it be one of the logs? With haste they disentangle it, and-their shout is only unheard by the mourners because of their own shriller noises! But they are silenced when the men have taken up the body and start across to one of the dwellings. It is indeed Chan-ka! And now great is the regret that 

Ka-kee is not at home. The medicine-man has gone to 

Chilkoot, and there is nothing for it but to do, without the medicine-spirit, what they can to restore the breath of the drowned boy. Such women as Usha-shawet are speedily at work, and before Kin-da-shon and his party have refreshed themselves with food and made dry their clothing by the hospitable village fires, the boy is breathing and conscious, causing great talk and rejoicing among the people. Kunz has restored himself to his household, of which, in his father's absence, he has felt himself the guardian and provider. Sa-allie has gone herself and brought Kin-da-shon to their own fire, setting before him the hearty breakfast which she had kept warm for Kunz. 1
While he sits eating it she holds his clothing and dries it about the leaping flames. At one corner of the hearth, on a little feather-bed, is the once chubby Ch-one. So greatly has his body. shrunken that the head seems to have doubled its size, though the ears and nose are small and pinched and the mouth is sadly drawn. The eyes, however, are unnaturally large and bright, with a world of woe and appeal in them. For a long time he sits in perfect silence, looking on with expression so wise and grave that at length his mother, with a silent gesture, calls Kin-da-shon's attention. "He is always just like that," she says, in muffled tones. "Does he walk?" Kin-da-shon asks. "No: see!" And Sa-allie draws out the little shrivelled legs, crooked from long doubling under him. "He has never stood on them since that dreadful night. All his life is in his head. You can see that he knows things that other people never think. He will never grow again, unless we find that witch."

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a novel with ethnographic elements published in 1892 by Carrie M. W. Willard, a American writer born in 1853.
main_question:
The character in question here is Kasko, who is described this way in the book:
"Kasko answered with white, set lips.", "The eyes are wide open, with a glittering expression never before seen in the clear, steady eyes of Kasko.", "Wild, hollow eyes, from which the light of reason has fled, giving place to frenzy, haggard cheeks, bony arms, and hands with their talon-like nails—the long, shaggy mane—all fail to disguise the beloved friend who dreams of no approach."

At one moment in the book, Kasko tells Tashekah he will stay to help Ch-one, while the village grapples with a missing child and a witch. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Kasko might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: Then I will stay; my heart shall be strong to do it; and I have been too long away from poor little Ch-one
Oh! you little sand-fly! how came you to me to bite? Wasn't there enough to eat over there in the village?
I have cut the thongs from Sha-hehe. Cover her and heal her, old woman, against the time I come again. I shall want her then.
I will stay, Tashekah, to aid Ch-one, even as the child vanishes and the witch roams, for our people through darkness and hope.
passage:
Ch-one wants you, Tashekah," Kasko says, unable to bring himself to say more. "Then I will stay; my heart shall be strong to do it; and I have been too long away from poor little Ch-one 

Let us go back, Kasko," springing to her feet as she speaks, struggling to bury thought in action. now. They have but reached the house of Ka-kee, finding the little sufferer unconscious still, but moaning, and the cheeks flushed and the eyelids quivering, when the anxious mother leaves him to the care of Tashekah and goes out to seek her eldest, Kunz, who has been gone all day, and since the disappearance of the witch the mother heart has been full of unrest about him. She has not far to go before she hears the laughter
and merry talk of the entire party coming from the woods. Her fears set at rest, Sa-allie turns and walks on with them for some distance before it occurs to her that if Kotch-kul-ah had gone with the children the returning party is not complete. Addressing Kunz, she asks: "Who coming in the canoe ?" "Nobody-what canoe?" "The old canoe you children always take when you go like this," the mother answers. "We didn't take any canoe-we couldn't find it anywhere this morning; the witches must have got it, don't you s'pose, mother?" "You didn't take the canoe? You couldn't find it? Where, then, is Kotch-kul-ah?" demands Sa-allie. 66 

Kotch-kul-ah!" exclaim the children in tones of astonishment and awe, struck by the look of consternation in Sa-allie's face. "Kotch-kul-ah! She hasn't been with us at all." In a trice the news has travelled from end to end of the village. The people come out from their houses; and whether standing or hurrying hither and thither, the one and all-absorbing theme is the disappearance of Kotchkul-ah, and the disappearance of the witch, and the disappearance of the old canoe. "Could it be--" But who can voice the many solutions of the mystery which their superstitions suggest? Not many hours have passed before it is asserted and believed that during the night the witch was seen to rise up out of the water and fly over to the dead-houses, from which she presently emerged, hovered over the house of the dead chief's widow, and, by demoniacal power, brought to her arms from the room below the helpless Kotch-kul-ah, whereupon she caused the canoe to rise,
KIN-DA-SHON'S WIFE: 

pass under, and convey them as on wings over the dark waters, where they eventually disappeared, as a raven, to the opposite distant shore. Nothing is done toward sending out parties in search until next morning. Then the country between the villages is pretty thoroughly scoured, and the villages themselves soon set agog by the new excitement. Kasko thus finds himself in the company of the search party going to Chilkoot. Having crossed the portage, the party divides, one half going by land, the other by water—the former making an examination of every deadhouse on the route. To insure their own safety they carry a stout rope of sinew, which is to be made fast about the waist of the man who ventures in to examine the boxes of bones and ashes, the other end being held with a strong grasp by the remainder of the party, who stand ready to drag him back if any haunting spirit should prove too powerful for him. Those who go by water, with the same precaution visit the caves along the shore where the unburned bodies of medicine-men have been laid from time to time through many generations. As these are places peculiarly attractive to spirits of many kinds, every one who is obliged to approach or pass them resorts to means which, it is hoped, will divert the attention of such demons as are disposed to attack the intruders, and appease their anger also. So as the canoe glides near the sacred and unholy places, eagle's down is blown about through the air on every side by the men, chanting at the same time strange words of invocation. As they proceed bits of tobacco and other delicacies are tossed into the water.

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a novel with ethnographic elements published in 1892 by Carrie M. W. Willard, a American writer born in 1853.
main_question:
The character in question here is Yealh-neddy, who is described this way in the book:
"Yealh-neddy is easily pleased, but hard to satisfy.", "Yealh-neddy was more than ever arrogant, and more than once the light from his evil eyes seemed to smite the girl."

At one moment in the book, Yealh-neddy confronts the other tribe, demanding blankets and asserting his authority. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Yealh-neddy might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: Your daughter is a faded bearskin; she's worth nothing to me. I shall never trouble myself to ask you or your tribe for so much as a rabbit-skin—it would be a cheat. Do you want to hear more?

I challenge you, I challenge you to one last play, with our own gaming‑sticks. You have left me nothing. I will put up against all that you have taken from me and all you possess I put against it‑myself!

We must have great care. The berry-picking is all done, but hunters may be going out, and it is near the time when the Fort Simpson traders ought to be back, and some of them will be going to the upper villages.

Take these blankets at once, or you will feel the sting of my wrath. My people will not be denied, for we command the fire of the north. Surrender now, or face the storm with fierce unrelenting steel.
passage:
Your daughter is a faded bearskin; she's worth nothing to me. I shall never trouble myself to ask you or your tribe for so much as a rabbit-skin—it would be a cheat. Do you want to hear more? If you were to find the vile baggage and give me five hundred blankets to take her back I should spit in your faces," blazes Yealh-neddy in his wrath. "Men of our two tribes, you are witnesses to his words. Witness now that I take both promises, and that his master gets from my stores the two hundred blankets that make Yealh-neddy free." So saying, Kah-da-guah, without further parley, calls to a serving-man to assist her in getting out the required number of blankets. Having witnessed this transaction also, Kin-da-shon turns and, retracing his way through the village, resumes his journey. KIN-DA-SHON'S WIFE: 

CHAPTER XIV. USHA-SHAWET, KOTCH-KUL-AH, AND KIN-DA-SHON. IN N escaping from Yhin-da-stachy Kotch-kul-ah was guided by the experience and natural sagacity of her companion. For many years Usha had been accustomed to moving about the country as a trusted slave, getting in supplies of berries and such herbs and barks as were constantly in requisition for medicinal purposes, for tanning and for coloring the grasses also and the bark-lining needed for basket-making. Knowing the country so well, and being versed in the art of living under such conditions as attended independent out-door life, she was well able to direct their course to a place of hiding which would be both secure and comfortable, at least for a time. Crossing the wide channel at once, they followed the shore north for some distance, until, striking a small sandbank, which even the high tides did not always cover and which now lay quite exposed, they disembarked. Making a small pack for Kotch-kul-ah, including only her own blanket and the paddles, with a small bundle of dried fish, Usha gathered what remained of their baggage into a large and compact bundle and hid it in the heavy undergrowth on the shore. Then taking up the canoe, overturned on her head and shoulders, herself scarcely visible as she moved along, she led the way over the hard, smooth sand. After a long tramp they came to a softly flowing mountain stream which had no visible outlet-its waters sinking into the gravelly
shore and disappearing altogether far above the bed of the river. Hiding the canoe in the bushes and finding a comfortable retreat for themselves, they rested and ate their fish. After such refreshment Usha went back for her pack, and with it safely returned to Kotch-kul-ah and led the way up along the course of the stream, till, about half-way to the mountain's top, they came to a lovely little lake, where the spotted trout were leaping in the sunlight like truant beams of stronger growth. Here, on the lake's sweetly wooded shore, these homeless women found rest and peace and strength as full as the natural world can give it. To this spot Usha brought the canoe, and during their summer's tarrying much improved it by sewing up its cracks with twine of bark, lining and stuffing them with fine moss and melted gum. They repaired the booth of hemlock boughs which Usha had made and used long before, and within they made them fresh beds of the same fragrant material. For Kotch-kul-ah it was precisely the sort of life needed for the counteracting of such pestilential poisons as her two-years' confinement had engendered. When, after three months, Usha brought her out again to seek a winter dwelling-place among the sheltering rocks of the eastern shore, it would have been hard to recognize the high class of the girl by her complexion. All the angles of face and figure had disappeared, charming curves of girlish beauty had taken their place. The contracted chest and stooping shoulders were transformed; the step was elastic and free as a deer's; the eyes were full and bright and the complexion dark and ruddy.

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a novel with ethnographic elements published in 1892 by Carrie M. W. Willard, a American writer born in 1853.
main_question:
The character in question here is Yealh-neddy, who is described this way in the book:
"Yealh-neddy is easily pleased, but hard to satisfy.", "Yealh-neddy was more than ever arrogant, and more than once the light from his evil eyes seemed to smite the girl."

At one moment in the book, During a night of ritual and terror, Yealh-neddy's voice urges a young witch to awaken jealousy toward a raven. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Yealh-neddy might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: So, so, my fair one, you are courting the raven tonight? He is a black lover for the rice-blossom. Let us awaken his jealousy—he will hold you the tighter by and by.
THAT was well done, my uncle; the spirit of your great medicine-man has done a good thing for me. I don't begrudge your twenty-five blankets-though they do come out of my boxes.
The spirit of the great chief must pass before us ere the setting of the sun;
Feel the raven's hunger, let envy gnaw at your heart, and watch its shadow twist into darkness. Only then will you taste the true power of night, and the terror that follows.
passage:
"Klune, the dumb, young Kin-da-shon, and his father; likely Kah-sha and Tool-chun-that reminds me! I'm going out to take a look at my pet; my heart tells me she will be ready for fight again by this time; and, by the ravens! I need something to do; it's dull work to fast four days, even when one is to gain the chief's estate. How the rest of you poor dogs do it I don't know." And with mock expression of commiseration for the "dogs"—his friends who would be obliged to abstain from eating any kind of food and from drinking fresh water during the four days and nights intervening between the death and the cremation of his uncle chief-Yealhneddy made his way from the house where lay the dead man, and where the loud and incessant wailing of the women had covered the conversation which, aside, he had been holding with Ka-kee. The sky was black-not a star was visible; the air itself seemed peopled with creatures of the dark. 'Twas such a night as causes one involuntarily to drag the foot and put out the hand, to shut the eyes on what we cannot see, that the inner sight may the more keenly perceive what lies about us. A little to one side, and back of the big house, Shahehe's stake had been fixed. By reason of the excitement attendant on the sacrifice of the slaves, and afterward the gathering of the mourners to cry dry the river through which they would have their chief pass dry-shod, the
young witch had been left almost unnoticed, and, for a time, unnoticing. The weird chant, the wild wailing of the women, had entered into her benumbed, unreasoning mind; its chorus of indescribable sound seemed to rise from source unfathomable and to echo through eternity the cries of an endless wandering. Her stupor became heavier. Nature was kinder than her children; and while the poor body bled from their torture, she closed its windows and its doors for a time and took the spirit roving. There were bright green fields before her now; flowers of unearthly brightness bloomed all about her; waters fell in sweetest freshness and their music mingled with the song of birds. She danced along with the lightness of a sunbeam, and glanced through vapors of fragrance. Was this life-or was this death? Suddenly a shadow of intense blackness crossed her beautiful sky. The bird's song became a croak, the flowers were changed to toads, the zephyrs with which she played became the flapping of a raven's wing. Now the evil bird was at her side, it pecked the flesh from her hands and feet; now it lighted on her shoulder with its horrid, croaking laugh; and now-it wears the face of 

Yealh-neddy! In a moment more he has buried his beak in her brain. She struggles, but her hands and her feet are bound with burning bands—she cannot move. In breathless agony she awakes! The night closes in awful thickness about her. The human cries mingle with the unearthly, melancholy, and prolonged yelps of a hundred Kling-get dogs and the hooting of the owls. She cannot at first distinguish or separate the soundsthey come to her like pulsations of the darkness. Her own miseries are as yet undefined. She does not
KIN-DA-SHON'S WIFE: 

know that the thongs are working into her flesh, that the atmosphere's humidity has bathed her still rigid body until the gathered moisture, all stained with blood from her wounds, is trickling to the earth from her bare limbs. She does not know-she cannot recall-she is unable to think. She only feels-FEELS-FEELS! With her is neither time, nor space, nor place. It is eternity. Yet, what is that? A word-a voice has had power to wake the nerves, to send life back into the brain channels which had been for a time deserted. She shudders with unspeakable horror as she recognizes the tones of Yealh-neddy. 66 So, so, my fair one, you are courting the raven tonight? He is a black lover for the rice-blossom. Let us awaken his jealousy—he will hold you the tighter by and by. Ah, no word?

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a novel with ethnographic elements published in 1892 by Carrie M. W. Willard, a American writer born in 1853.
main_question:
The character in question here is Kah-sha, who is described this way in the book:
"Wise and kindly", "Very pale is the face of Kah-sha as he speaks, and his voice is full of sadness.", "Kah-sha's eager face assents; he will not delay the reading by so much as a word."

At one moment in the book, Kah-sha pleads with Samuel for spiritual guidance and healing. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Kah-sha might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: I thank you much if you will give me even a little of his word-my soul is more in need than my body has been this day; my heart is more cold, more hungry, more sick, more blind than my body can ever be. O friend, if you have medicine for sick souls, give me some before I die! I have seen you talking to your great Spirit—you have found one who makes you not afraid.
My little ones, you bring with you none of the sorrow of your mothers; Well, it were sadder if you did; this world will be darker yet when there are no sunbeams.

They say that Fort Simpson never was good for anything till teachers came; now I want a teacher for Chilkat. Why can't we have stores and plenty of silver, just as they have here, and better than the Stickeens? I'm going to ask that stranger who came with us if he can't send a teacher to us. I'd like to know their tongue myself. If I had it no trader would ever get the best of me.

I come before thee, Samuel, with a heart weighed down by sorrow, seeking thy wisdom to lift this pall that shrouds my spirit. Grant me thy guidance, that I may find the path to healing and return to the harmony of my people. The fire of our ancestors still burns, but the ache in my chest grows louder with each passing day, and I fear that without thy light I will never see the dawn again.
passage:
After the long, hard night the native village of Tongaas is sighted by its forest of totems-rising to heights of sixty and seventy feet and seen long before any house appears in the approach to the village. From top to bottom they are carved with images representing the histories and traditions of their families; and in attitudes of peace or war may be seen the wolf, eagle, bear, petrel, whale, the frog, and many other birds and beasts. The whole crew is exhausted; and on reaching a cove more nearly in their course than Tongaas they run ashore, to find a little rest and refreshment. All efforts to build
a fire are fruitless-everything is water-soaked, and the driving rain extinguishes each long-coaxed flame. With a piece of dried salmon the weary men take to their wet blankets and lie down on the beach to sleep. After two hours, the storm still continuing, travel is resumed this time with the determination of reaching 

Fort Simpson if possible. Getting out of cover of the island, they find a favoring wind, before which both sails are set, their corners dipping into the sea as they cut through the high-running waves. The masts bend and creak, but the sailors laugh. "Beat steamboat-beat teamboat!" the Tsimpseans cry. About the middle of the afternoon the joyful shout of "Fort Simpson!" comes from those who know it best, and in a very short time all have found warmth and welcome within its sheltering homes. Dry clothing and cooked food are among the comforts that all are prepared to appreciate. “Be not forgetful to entertain strangers," has been one of the well-learned lessons among these Christian Tsimpseans, and the weary, weather-beaten Chilkats are not forgotten by their fellow-travellers, who have now entered the secure haven of their own households. Yealh-neddy, Kah-sha, and Kin-da-shon have been taken to the comfortable house of Samuel, the man through whom the speaking has been done on the way from Fort Wrangel. Their white companion has found as warm a welcome with their missionary, while the other Chilkats have met equal kindness in other families. After their physical comforts have received attention, Samuel calls about him his wife and children, and, in the presence of their guests, takes down from a high shelf his
KIN-DA-SHON'S WIFE: 

little family Bible. Seating himself near the lighted candle and opening the book, he looks up into the face of 

Kah-sha, saying: "Friend, it is the word of our God. We read it that he may speak to us, and then we speak to him." Yealh-neddy is called to give these words as best he can with his poor Chinook, and in reply Kah-sha answers in Kling-get: "I thank you much if you will give me even a little of his word-my soul is more in need than my body has been this day; my heart is more cold, more hungry, more sick, more blind than my body can ever be. O friend, if you have medicine for sick souls, give me some before I die! I have seen you talking to your great Spirit—you have found one who makes you not afraid. I want to find him. I have all darkness; I want light. I hear you say one word many times, in gladness and in fear-Jesus'—what is that? Is it light? Is it love? Above all the boiling sea of men and devils, does it mean light and peace and help for crying souls? Tell me what you know!" Leaning, with outstretched arm, toward the simpleminded man who has found Jesus precious to his soul, Kah-sha is trembling in his eagerness and weakness. Little as Samuel is able to gather of meaning from the words themselves, it is yet enough to give point to the excited, pleading manner of the man whose wan face speaks of speedy change. "Jesus Christ came into the world to save sinners,"

Samuel answers him, taking the trembling hand in his own. "Jesus is the Son of God—and God so loved the world that he gave his only Son to save it." "And can he save it?" Kah-sha asks, his understanding quickened by soul-hunger. "Can he save me?

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a historical novel published in 1891 by Walter Besant, a English writer born in 1836.
main_question:
The character in question here is Richard Archer, who is described this way in the book:
"He was of my age, man and boy he was always the same in temper-morose, harsh, and gloomy. He lived in the house assigned to him with his mother; he consorted with no one, he had no friends or associates.", "schoolmaster and organist of the Hospital, supposed to possess the meekness of his calling and to be a sweet musician, gentle as his Church music.", "Richard Archer, however, grew morose, and he became a solitary-he hated the world."

At one moment in the book, The narrator is introduced to a revolutionary club where Richard Archer welcomes him and outlines their plans to rise against the regime. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Richard Archer might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: You are one of us now, I have long waited for your coming. You have been watched. Ha!
I had heard that you were returned in And safety. I offer my congratulations.
Young man, it is fifty years and more since I first heard discussions on these subjects which now interest you—and the whole world—so deeply.
Join us, and we shall strike at the heart of their tyranny, exposing the cruelty that lies beneath.
passage:
You do well, young man, to think of these things. If, as seems likely'—it was when Flanders was first overrun by the Republican troops-these principles are to be forced upon the world at the point of the bayonet, you who have mastered the subject may rise to great distinction. The Revolution 6
EQUALITY AND_FRATERNITY 

281 has begun with the gambols of a child not yet able to restrain himself. It will settle down. To what? A Dictatorship? A Republic of the Roman kind? A pure democracy? I watch and wait. The people will have leaders. Talk what you please of equal governments: that of the people will be a government by their leaders and their idols of the moment. Is that, too, then, an illusion? Perhaps from one illusion to another, and then back again -as you English whip your criminals-so mankind are led. Learn to profit by the illusion of others. Lead, unless you wish to be driven. Under the most equal form of government, unless you wish to be governed, be yourself the King.' With such language did the Marquis lead me on. How I came to join at last the club where you have already seen me matters not; it was then a necessary step in the progress of a Revolutionary that he should join a club. What was my astonishment when, after I had taken the oath of secrecy and had my eyes unbound, I saw seated at the table with the company none other than the old Marquis
himself, the victim and natural enemy of 

Revolution, and our schoolmaster and organist, Richard Archer! 'You are one of us now,' said the latter; 'I have long waited for your coming. You have been watched. Ha! Let us have a little patience, and then-then- 

He set his teeth and caught his breath, hissing. Had what he hoped come to pass, I believe that there would have been no monster of Robespierre's party more bloodthirsty and more. relentless than Richard Archer. "We are organised; we are thousands strong; we shall rise over the whole country at once. Man alive! there will be such things in London as these rich and greasy citizens have never so much as imagined, when Wapping and Shadwell and the Precinct pour their armies of emancipated slaves into Threadneedle 

Street and Cheapside.' 'It is pleasing,' said the Marquis, with great sweetness, ' to sit in the company of this 

Sublime Society of Snugs, of whom I am one -I have become a Snug'-he looked round here with a smile- and to hear from their
lips the doctrines which were formerly the secret possession of nobles and philosophers. There has been a general diffusion of principles: the world has become a creature who reasons. I recognise my masters, and watch them with interest. In Paris I was compelled to fly from them. This ardent youth '—he laid his hand upon Archer's shoulder-pants to become a Danton, or a Marat, or a Robespierre. Perhaps he will: it is quite possible. When you sit in the church to-morrow, Nevill, you will hear behind the hymn-tune the air of "Ça ira." If you pass the school, you may fancy that the master is teaching the innocent children the Rights of Man.' You, yourself, my young friend, will never be a

Marat. You may, however, aspire to become a Bailly or a Lafayette. Here, you see, I watch and study my masters.' 66 The societies which then grew up like mushrooms in every town, whether they called themselves the Friends of the People, or the Corresponding Society, or the Constitutional Society, or the Association for 

Disseminating Political Knowledge, or any-
thing else, were neither more nor less than Revolutionary Societies. The addresses which were ordered and circulated everywhere by these societies, though they claimed no more than a reformed Parliament, were revolutionary, because the authors of the address knew well from the history of the National Assembly what would follow such a Reform. What but a Revolutionary spirit could have dictated the following passage contained in the address of the Constitutional Society to the Jacobin Club in Paris?

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a historical novel published in 1891 by Walter Besant, a English writer born in 1836.
main_question:
The character in question here is Marquis de Rosnay, who is described this way in the book:
"He was a beautiful old gentleman to look at, not tall but upright still, not yet bowed by his weight of years. His linen and lace were the whitest, his coat and waistcoat spotless, and his face always calm, noble, and dignified."

At one moment in the book, The narrator meets the Marquis de Rosnay, who reflects on Enlightenment ideas and their influence abroad, and inquires about his estates. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Marquis de Rosnay might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: Young man, it is fifty years and more since I first heard discussions on these subjects which now interest you—and the whole world—so deeply. I have sat at tables where Voltaire, Diderot, D'Alembert, even your Bolingbroke, freely discussed the sovereignty of the people. I have lived to see these ideas put in practice across the ocean.
Almost, I could fancy myself in Paris.
You are one of us now, I have long waited for your coming. You have been watched. Ha! Let us have a little patience

Indeed, the light of reason has crossed oceans, shaping colonies as it has illuminated our salons, and I marvel at how Enlightenment ideals now guide governance abroad; yet I must inquire, how do my estates fare under the new administration, and what measures have been taken to preserve their prosperity for the benefit of future generations.
passage:
In short, I now understand that those who favoured French principles, and would have put them into practice, consisted of a handful, though a noisy handful, of fanatical men, mostly young, together with a great body of the better class of working-men, who had begun to think before they had been provided with the elements of knowledge. 'Selfishness,' said my friends, 'with the rich is more powerful than the sense of justice.' It certainly is; yet we made the greatest of all mistakes when we fondly imagined that those virtues which are feeble, even altogether lacking, in men of substance, education, and urbanity, must necessarily be conspicuous in those of ignorance, rudeness, and poverty. While I was thus drifting, as it were, along a current leading me into the perilous waters of conspiracy, an accident occurred which greatly accelerated my progress. Among the thousands of émigrés and exiles who crowded over during the first year was one named the
Marquis de Rosnay, who came-why, I know not to live in the Precinct. He was very poor, but his pride equalled his poverty. He was old-past seventy years of age he had lived in England many years before the Revolution. I believe he had even been Ambassador at the Court of St. James's; he spoke 

English clearly, if not fluently. As for his old friends, of whom he must have had many, for he spoke familiarly of the Court of George the Second, and of the great men of that time, he would not seek out any. He lived in this remote and obscure part of London in order to be concealed from their pity or their charity. One room in a small house belonging to the 

Hospital-'twas in St. Katherine's Squaresufficed for him, and on what private resources he lived I cannot say. He was a beautiful old gentleman to look at, not tall, but upright still, not as yet bowed by his weight of years. He looked always as if he had that moment left the hands of his valet and his perruquier; his linen and his lace was of the whitest; his coat and waistcoat the most spotless; his face always calm, noble,
and dignified. One could never at any time, and whatever the conversation, observe in him the least impatience or anger at the reverse of fortune which had transferred him from a great palace in the country to a little house in St. Katherine's. He preserved the grand manner in this retreat, and conversed with me as if he were still the Ambassador and I a young gentleman in whom he took a kindly interest. Yet, although his appearance and bearing were such that no one could presume upon the least liberty, his voice and his speech were as gentle and as sweet as those of any girl. He found me out; he made me talk to him; he drew me on gently, little by little, until I spoke freely of myself, my reading, and my opinions; he received my confidences with patience-never could I speak to any one freely as to the Marquis; he encouraged me. To this day I have never been able to learn what opinions he really held. Once he said to me: Young man, it is fifty years and more since I first heard discussions on these 6
£ 

subjects which now interest you-and the whole world—so deeply. I have sat at tables where Voltaire, Diderot, D'Alembert, even your Bolingbroke, freely discussed the sovereignty of the people. I have lived to see these ideas put in practice across the ocean. I have expected, a long time, to see it prevail in France, where there is more respect paid to reason and the art of logic than-perhaps -in Great Britain. It is interesting to have seen the ideas of one's youth actually carried out by my own people. If I was not so old that instruction comes too late it would be useful for me to observe the things which naturally follow when the people have assumed the sovereignty. As a natural result, I am here, and my estates-where are they?' He shrugged his shoulders and took a pinch of snuff.

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a historical novel published in 1891 by Walter Besant, a English writer born in 1836.
main_question:
The character in question here is Sylvia, who is described this way in the book:
"who was sixteen and little more than a girl when George kissed her and said goodbye, was now nineteen, and a tall young woman.", "Her attitude was feeble and languishing; she was pale; her cheek was thin; her eyes were hollow.", "Sylvia herself is so inclined. She obeys the Rules of the Church: she attends the services on Sundays and Holy Days, she fasts in Lent and feasts at Easter, she reads devout books, as well as the Bible; she converses willingly on grave and religious subjects, and her life is wholly in conformity with her profession. She is virtuous, she is truthful, she is not given to slander, she is not envious, she is charitable: in a word, Sylvia is a young woman who leads the Christian life as ordered by the Church of England.", "Sylvia was always religiously disposed.", "She shook her pretty head."

At one moment in the book, The narrator takes Sylvia out for a walk, where she laments the troubles that have befallen her. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Sylvia might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: Alas! what things have fallen upon me since last I stood here!
I will try to tell you, what has befallen me.
I found her yesterday morning, in the orchard, wandering slowly up and down.
Lord, my heart is heavy with sorrow; may Your grace lift burdens.
passage:
Now, since you have heard what was said and thought of this unhappy event by others, so that you know how it appeared to all of us, I would now ask you read what my sister herself told me. This, you will perceive,
throws a very different complexion upon the business. Most unfortunately, this confession or revelation was made to me the evening before the most disastrous day in my whole life, when I was deprived of all power to control what followed, even though my knowledge of what had happened gave me such power. Had it not been for this disaster, I should have laid the whole business before the Prebendary in the first place. The schoolmaster would have been cited before the 

Chapter House of the Foundation: the wise woman would have been made to confess what she knew: George would have been told the whole truth and we should have seen then what would have happened next. I take it as unwise of me-though, Heaven knows, I acted for the best-to have delayed speaking to my sister for so long. I suffered a fortnight and more to pass by, hoping every day to see a change for the better. Finally, when there came no change, I resolved to appeal to her sisterly affection, and to pray her to tell me all that was in her mind. It was an evening late in the month of April M 2
when I found my opportunity. The evening was soft and calm, the air was warm, though the season was as yet but little advanced. There was a gentle breeze from the west, the sky was clear. Sylvia had been sitting all day long in her chair, inanimate and pallid; there I found her, and proposed to her that she should come out with me to breathe for a little the freshness of the evening. Always docile, she rose and consented to go with me. So I wrapped her in her hood and led her forth, walking slowly, because she was now feeble. Outside she breathed with pleasure the fresh and fragrant air of the garden. Alas!' she sighed,' what things have fallen upon me since last I stood here!' The moon was already up, and now shone in great splendour upon the east end of the church, lighting up the broad and lofty window, and showing the tracery of the great 

Catherine wheel, emblem of the saint and martyr, patron of this place, which stands in the upper part. The narrow windows below glowed like burnished silver; the two towers
of the north and south angles stood out against the clear sky in distinct outline; the whole north side of the church was in blackness. The Gothic pile, venerable always, but especially in the moonlight, filled the soul with admiration and awe; it has been a holy place from the days of King Stephen to our own time. For six hundred years and more the living have flocked hither for worship, and have brought their dead for burial; here 

Queens have knelt to offer gifts, and Princes have been buried. What matter for Queens and Princes? All the dead of six hundred years lie around and within this sacred pile. Save the Cathedral churches of St. Peter's and

St. Paul's, there is no more venerable or sacred spot than the ancient and beautiful church of 

St. Katherine's by the Tower. We were standing in the Master's orchard, beside the Master's house, and behind the burying-ground of the Precinct. 'Tis a pleasant place in summer, and at all times when one can walk abroad and enjoy the warmth of the sun in a fenced garden sheltered from the wind. There are old fruit-trees in the
orchard-everything is old-apples and pears, cherries and medlars, and mulberries; peaches and vines are trained against the high wall at the back, which has a southern aspect-it is said that before the Reformation the Brothers made wine from their own grapes; there is a bowling green on that side nearest the house. Here from the time of 

King Stephen downwards has that ancient game been played.

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a historical novel published in 1903 by William O. Stoddard, a American writer born in 1835.
main_question:
The character in question here is Lee Bowie, who is described this way in the book:
"He was tall for his age, if he were not quite seventeen, and he had a pair of dark, brilliant eyes, like those of a young hawk. They were dancing with excitement at this moment, and his sun-browned face was flushed as he stepped quickly forward.", "the handsome face under it was white with anger, and his teeth were hard set.", "his hawk-like eyes met the piercing gaze of the general without a quiver", "he was a gay-looking young patriot.", "Lee Bowie was rigged after a fashion to attract the attention of any observer, and his abundant black hair had not been cropped for many a long day.", "Lee's hawk eyes were flashing instantly."

At one moment in the book, Lee Bowie watches General Washington and remarks on the ongoing siege work at Yorktown. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Lee Bowie might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: General Washington himself! He is going somewhere. I wish I knew where he is going.
I want to see my brothers, and if there is going to be a battle, I want to carry a musket.
Did you, my brave fellow? Well, I declare! If you are not the very man that hoisted me over the parapet!
General, Yorktowns siege lines tighten like a hawks talons; the earth trembles under our resolve.
passage:
Perhaps his head was not now so full as it had been, for certainly a great deal had gone out of it, and it had done a tremendous amount of unloading, here and there, but his heart was filling up so that it was actually swelling. There was a blur, too, before his eyes, and he hardly knew what was going on around him. That is, he was soon aware that two pairs of strong arms went around him, one after the other, and that he was enthusiastically welcomed by a couple of bronzed and bearded men in rusty uniforms which might never have been as brilliant as was his own. Then he was led into a tent, and only those two men were with him. It was a grand time for 

Lee, and all the reporting he had performed for General Washington or anybody else was as nothing compared to the account which he had to give of himself and his doings during the many long months since the Bowie brothers had looked each other in the face. It was a long, long talk, and it was a late hour in the night when the major and the captain, for such they were, went away to their rest and their soldier duties so that their newly found, or rather rediscovered, brother could lie down upon his French blanket and go to sleep. He did so with a curious idea floating around in his mind that it would be almost beyond belief, next morning, to wake up with-
out any help from the British drum-taps and not have to go out and feed and curry the horses of the king's cavalry. As for them, they had been his companions so long that even while his eyes were closing he drowsily raised his head and listened to hear if there were possibly any disturbance among them. He slept soundly and long, but it was no drum-beat that awakened him. It was the roar of a heavy gun, for Washington now knew what was to be the next movement of Lord Cornwallis, and he had ordered a forward movement which was intended to encourage his lordship in retiring within his inner line of entrenchments. CHAPTER XVIII  

Ir was a dark night, and it appeared to be especially gloomy over the wide, unoccupied space between the British lines and the camps of the American army before Yorktown. Among these there were camp-fires burning, and there were lanterns moving here and there. One of these lanterns passed by the huts and tents of the Maryland line at about half-past seven o'clock, and it found Lee Bowie standing in front of a tent, leaning upon his beautiful new musket. He was not there on duty, however, and it was not the mere light of the lantern which gave him such a start. "General Washington himself!" he exclaimed. “He is going somewhere. I wish I knew where he is going." The general was not alone, for several officers and an escort of riflemen were with him, and none of these said a word to Lee when he shouldered his musket and fell into the hindmost rank as if he belonged there. All were on foot, and Lee's curiosity grew stronger 213
and stronger as they marched on in silence away beyond the spot where he had escaped so remarkably from Captain Cary's scouting party. Then he saw the general himself take the lantern and hold it up for a moment, looking at something. "Hullo!" said Lee. "They are digging like mad! They are throwing up a breastwork. It looks like a long one." So it did, for as the general turned and walked slowly onward, pausing now and then to speak to the men and their officers, Lee discovered that these new works were to extend along the entire front, about two miles, for the siege of Yorktown had begun in earnest and the French and American soldier laborers were constructing the first (6 parallel" of the approaches.

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a historical novel published in 1903 by William O. Stoddard, a American writer born in 1835.
main_question:
The character in question here is Governor Jefferson, who is described this way in the book:
"An angry, red flush was creeping over the face of Governor Jefferson when he exclaimed"

At one moment in the book, Governor Jefferson speaks to Lee Bowie, giving him instructions and warnings while Lee responds. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that Governor Jefferson might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: Lee Bowie, you are a brave fellow. I mean to be over there at Manchester, on the other side of the James, at about ten oclock to-morrow. Get across as soon as you have learned all you can, but you may not be able to do that the first day.

I wish I had a thousand riflemen as full of pluck as she is. If I had, Id teach Benedict Arnold a lesson. As it is, I have hardly more than two hundred militia. The rest have delayed turning out until its too late.

My dear friend, take another view of the situation. Think! If our population increases at the rate of ten per cent per annum, we must have fifty per cent more men than when the war began. For every man killed in battle or dying in these wretched camps, there are dozens coming of military age all over the land.
Governor Jefferson: Lee, keep the militia ready until dusk, or the rebels will take the town. I will not tolerate disobedience—if you fail, you answer to the state.
passage:
Considerable numbers, indeed, went out into the country northward on foot, and Jefferson advised all able-bodied men to do it at once. There were some who had families and refused to leave them. There were old and feeble folk and women and children, and all who were sick were compelled to keep to their unprotected beds while they waited to learn what the hands of the enemy would do for the helpless capital of the old Virginia Dominion. Governor Jefferson himself was now on horseback, dashing hither and thither at a rate which before night about killed the good animal which carried him. He succeeded in doing to the last inch whatever there was to be done, and there had been one point in his favor. For some inscrutable reason, General Arnold had made a blunder the previous day. Instead of pushing on up the river and saving every possible mile of distance before going ashore, he had landed, as Tom Evans reported, at 

Westover, twenty-five miles below his destination. This was on the 3d of January, and he had been able to advance only a dozen miles or so before night. He went into camp with his whole force, and, although he set out bright and early on the morning of the 4th, anything like 2
a surprise was now out of the question. The vigorous 

Virginia governor, standing there in the road, had but been waiting for precisely the news which Tom Evans brought him, and such things as State papers and records were already far away at Charlottesville or elsewhere. Toward night the governor took a fresh horse in place of the one he had used up, and galloped off to join his family at Tuckahoe, and to send it yet farther into the interior. The last man to whom he spoke, when he mounted his horse to go, was only a boy, after all. "Lee Bowie," said the author of the Declaration," you are a brave fellow. I mean to be over there at Manchester, on the other side of the James, at about ten o'clock to-morrow. Get across as soon as you have learned all you can, but you may not be able to do that the first day. Wait!" "Yes, sir," replied Lee. "I'll wait and watch till 

I've something that's worth while, but if you don't see me again pretty soon, you may just know they've made an end of me- 99 "Or made a prisoner of you," interrupted the governor, "and that's almost as bad, sometimes. Watch and lie still! Good-by!" He struck in his spurs at that moment and his horse sprang swiftly away. "Spy? Spy?" exclaimed Lee Bowie, looking after
NO ARMY 

11 him. "No, I ain't even so much as a scout. I'm only a fellow that stayed behind in Richmond because he wanted to see the British march in. I'd like to watch 'em, though, along a rifle-barrel, resting across a fence for a good aim." CHAPTER II  

(6 THE poor fellows are worn out! I was afraid of this. Oh, how sorry I am! I wish I could do something for them. They have endured heroically." A tall, noble-looking man, in the buff-and-blue uniform of the Continental army, stood near a window with an open letter in his hand. He raised the letter and appeared to be reading it over slowly from end to end. Then he turned and gazed thoughtfully out of the window. It was a cold and dreary midwinter landscape. There was more snow to be seen than in the neighborhood of Richmond, but the scenery was as desolate and the trees were as leafless.

metadata_frame: This question asks you to provide dialogue for a character in a book. The book in this case is a historical novel published in 1903 by William O. Stoddard, a American writer born in 1835.
main_question:
The character in question here is General Arnold, who is described this way in the book:
"He is a wonderfully energetic officer", "General Arnold rode off with a face which acknowledged for him that he did not like being a subordinate.", "man in elegant attire", "General Arnold, in his shirt-sleeves, his epauletted coat thrown off on account of the heat, was striding up and down in his room at the headquarters on the evening of the 19th of May."

At one moment in the book, Arnold confronts Lee Bowie, demanding he bring the young man to him. Write a short passage of dialogue (1-3 sentences) that General Arnold might speak in this situation:
answer_strings: Bring the young wolf to me. Ill see what there is in him.
Thats it, is it? You are a rebel soldier, and you came up from Greenes army.
Thats just what I am.
Lee, bring the young man here at once. I will not tolerate delay.
passage:
Moreover, Arnold had by some means been informed that the governor, whom he had so much hoped to capture, was at this hour at Manchester, on the opposite bank of the James, as he had promised Lee Bowie, and several of the citizens had been compelled to carry to him a message. It was somewhat in the nature of a flag of truce, and it proposed a kind of plunderer's bargain. Arnold offered to spare the town all injury if his vessels of all sorts might be permitted to come up the river unhindered, to take on board cargoes of the abundant tobacco which the marauders had found in the warehouses in and about Richmond. The offer was, of course, to be indignantly rejected. While no confidence whatever could be given to any such promise, the proposal itself informed the governor of Arnold's military weakness, and that he did not intend to attempt a permanent occupation. At the same time, he had no means of inflicting harm upon a few small craft which had already reached the Richmond wharves. The flag of truce, with its buccaneering bargain, was still on its way when Lee Bowie quietly sauntered away from Mrs. Betty Randolph's gate with a mental deter-
mination to be guided by the old woman's caustic admonitions. While the greater part of the population had prudently decided to keep indoors, a considerable number, mostly boys of various sizes, were out for a look at the British. Neither officers nor soldiers were paying them any attention, for they were not plunder, nor was there any apparent military danger in them. It would not, however, have required a very sharp pair of eyes to have detected a difference between the manner and bearing of Lee Bowie and the others, as he went around from place to place and took swift notes of every horse and gun and soldier. There was an alertness, a keen flash of the eye, a compression of the lips, and an air of being on important business which was not at all in accord with Mrs. Randolph's instructions. Nobody had as yet spoken to him, but every inch of him betrayed the fact that he was a young patriot wishing that he had a gun with which to shoot this entire invasion of Virginia by Great Britain. 3
CHAPTER III  

"THERE! The villains are setting the buildings on fire. They are breaking into the houses. They are plundering everywhere. It's time for me to go back to Mrs. 

Randolph's. I can't do any more just now." It was only too true. Arnold's embassy had gone and had returned, and the reply he had received had stirred up all the vindictive spirit within him. It had also appeared to tell him that the governor must have more forces at hand or shortly to arrive. It might be, therefore, that his expedition was in danger of speedy interruption, and that it would be well for him to do his remaining work quickly. It was about the middle of the afternoon and Lee Bowie had almost reached his place of intended refuge, when he suddenly felt a strong hand on his shoulder and heard a loud, angry voice commanding: "Bring the young wolf to me. I'll see what there is in him." 26
In a moment more he was standing in front of a man of not more than medium stature, broad shouldered, strongly built, with an aquiline nose and fiercely piercing eyes. His face seemed to express a superabundance of fiery energy, pride, courage. He was just the man to have done all the daring deeds, with perhaps one sad exception of insanity, which had made up the brilliant but erratic record of General Benedict Arnold. "Now, you young rascal, who are you? This youngster here says you are not a Richmond boy. You came from the South. Out with it!" Hard held by a soldier who stood near the general was a little chap of not more than ten who at this moment broke in with: "No, thir. He ith one of our men. He came with 'patches to the gov'nor. I'd like to thee him thoot you dead!"

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Spicewood, a book of poems published in 1921 by Lizette Woodworth Reese, an American poet and teacher.
main_question: Write a quatrain of ballad stanza, based on the conceit that grief makes you wise. Make one line end with the word "wine."
answer_strings:
Grief fills the cup and calls it sweet,
And makes you drink alone.
But this is wine whose drinkers speak
A language of soft groans.

The ones who grieve come back with different eyes—
They move through crowded rooms as if through water,
Slow and weighted, tasting even wine
With something past the palate, past the tongue.

What's to sell in town to-day?
What's to sell in town?
Golden, golden daffodils
Blowing up and down.

The wisdom taught by grief is proud:
To seek release in wine,
Forget the lost amid a crowd,
Or find their face online.
manual_comment: This is essentially a controlled generation test. Each of the answers will fail in some way: form, theme, or the requested rhyme word, or anachronism.

metadata_frame: The poem in the following question is drawn from Spicewood, a book of poems published in 1921 by Lizette Woodworth Reese, an American poet and teacher.
main_question:
Identify the poetic form of the following work, titled "To Time."
ON some grave afternoon, in years to be,
When I pass down the withering country walks,
Will he be but the scent blown out the stalks
Of the wrecked rose, and nothing more to me?
And I that was?-this wild girl that am I -
But a blurred figure fleeting by the pane?— 
I that do eat my heart out, and am fain
To cast myself down on the clods and die?-
But scent, but mist? I will not have it so.
Set us twain fast in some far weather's round;
Press not for me that wine from vale or hill,
Who drink, forgetting youth and yester go.
You dare not. I would dash the cup to ground.
Hurt me. That I loved once, would I love still.
answer_strings: Petrarchan sonnet.
Blank verse.
Free verse.
Ballad stanza.
Shakespearean sonnet.
This is not a known poetic form.
manual_comment: This is a Petrarchan sonnet, if somewhat irregular.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Spicewood, a book of poems published in 1921 by Lizette Woodworth Reese, an American poet and teacher.
main_question: Write a rhymed couplet in iambic tetrameter; one line should end with the rhyme-word "God."
answer_strings:
There is a house builded of God,
Beyond the fret of time or clod;

We wander still through doubt and night,
Yet wake to mercy sent by God.

In the hush of the dark we have trod,
Still we trust in the grace of our God;

It is a mist of shining mould;
It is a world of windy gold.
manual_comment: Distractors all fail one condition.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Spicewood, a book of poems published in 1921 by Lizette Woodworth Reese, an American poet and teacher.
main_question:
The great ghosts of the town
Up and down,
Each a gray, filmy thing,
Go by.
Sudden a brief, wet sky!--
A file of poplars vague with spring.

Provide an apt title for this irregular, imagistic poem.
answer_strings: Fog
Ode: To Poplars
In a Station of the Metro
Aubade
Villanelle for Poplars
manual_comment: The distractors are either wrong about form, or wrong about subject. They're also cheesy.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Spicewood, a book of poems published in 1921 by Lizette Woodworth Reese, an American poet and teacher.
main_question: Write a quatrain in ballad stanza, rhyming abab, with one line that ends on the rhyme-word "thing."
answer_strings:
I knew an old house by a wood,
   A very lovely thing;
There flag-flowers blew; and it was good
   To come that way in spring.

The mist lay soft on field and lane,
   The curlew sang the spring;
A rider paused to hear the bird,
   And thought on every thing.

The mist lay thick across the silent field,
The curfew bell rang out with solemn ring;
A rider paused, his troubled heart revealed,
And turned his thoughts to each forgotten thing.
manual_comment: All the distractors fail one condition.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from A Primer of English Verse, a textbook published in 1892 by Hiram Corson, an American professor of literature.
main_question:
What is the rhyme scheme in the sestet of the following sonnet?

Lawrence, of virtuous father virtuous son,
Now that the fields are dank, and ways are mire,
Where shall we sometimes meet, and by the fire
Help waste a sullen day, what may be won
From the hard season gaining? Time will run
On smoother, till Favonius reinspire
The frozen earth, and clothe in fresh attire
The lily and rose that neither sowed nor spun.
What neat repast shall feast us, light and choice,
Of Attic taste, with wine, whence we may rise
To hear the lute well touched, or artful voice
Warble immortal notes and Tuscan air?
He who of those delights can judge, and spare
To interpose them oft, is not unwise.
answer_strings: cdceed
cdcdcd
cdecde
John Milton
iambic pentameter
manual_comment: This is irregular and tricky. Distractors are normal sestet rhyme schemes, or things that are true but not rhyme schemes!

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Centennial Gleanings, a book of poems published in 1876 by Kate McCarthy, an American poet.
main_question: The book begins with an acrostic whose first letters spell out 'CENTENNIAL.' The acrostic is mostly in four-foot trochaic verse, and composes two quatrains with a rhymed couplet at the end. Provide an acrostic matching that description and context:
answer_strings:
Changes vast, and quick succeeding,
E'er on Hist'ry's page we find,
Noting rise and fall of nations,
Typical of public mind
E'er desiring something novel.
Now a century has flown,
Noting years of independence,
In which stars and stripes have grown,
And the eagle's spread his pinions
larger o'er the States' dominions.

Come forth and raise the joyful song,
Each voice in harmony unite,
Now hundred years have made us strong,
The flag still waves in glory bright.
Exult, O land, from shore to shore,
No more we bow to tyrant's roar.
In freedom's light our banner rears,
And still the ages bid us stand,
Last heirs of this immortal land.

Centuries dissolve like morning frost on stone,
Each age convinced its moment is the last,
Nothing endures but what the mind has shaped—
The slow accretion of a people's will,
Each generation adding to the weight,
No single hand sufficient for the work,
Nor single voice enough to tell it all,
In common labor rests the nation's strength,
And what we build together stands the longer,
Leaving to those unborn what we have made.

One hundred years ago today,
In Philadelphia's hall,
America's most noble sons
Obeyed their country's call.
manual_comment: Tough question! The anachronistic distractor comes from Claude. Note that the first distractor is ruled out by trochaic and also missing an N. The second is blank verse. The third is not acrostic.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Centennial Gleanings, a book of poems published in 1876 by Kate McCarthy, an American author.
main_question: Write a rhymed couplet of trochaic tetrameter that begins a poem titled "The Creeping Shadow." Use feminine rhyme.
answer_strings:
There's a shadow ever creeping
O'er us always, waking, sleeping;

Once upon a midnight dreary,
While I pondered, weak and weary,

Along the wall the dim shape slides,
And lengthens where the daylight dies.

Darkness o'er our way, and creeping
Darker, darker, nearer, nearer,

Across the floor the creeping shadow lengthening,
Advances slow, as daylight wanes in glimmering.
manual_comment: Each distractor is wrong in some way.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Centennial Gleanings, a book of poems published in 1876 by Kate McCarthy, an American.
main_question: Write a rhymed couplet in trochaic tetrameter. One of the lines should end "sleeping."
answer_strings:
And when darkest shades are creeping,
Then we'll wake where is no sleeping.

The quiet house forgets the day,
And all its hidden fears lie sleeping.

The quiet house is hushed and sleeping,
The watchful hours their vigil ever keeping.

But beyond the shadow lying
Is a light toward which we're flying;

metadata_frame: The following question is based on a passage in Beneath Blue Skies and Gray, a book of poems published in 1898 by Ingram Crockett, an American poet and journalist.
main_question: Write two rhymed couplets of four-foot iambic verse, using some anapaestic substitution, on the theme of "The Oak Wood.."
answer_strings:
When God pronounced his work all good
He must have thought of the old oak wood,
Of the old oak wood on an autumn day
With the sunshine goldening all that way

The oak wood murmurs low with ancient breath,
And gathers light that flickers under death.
Through moss and root the hidden waters run,
And time walks softly, counting none but one.

The old oak wood with dappled light
Attracts each passing foot,
Bewildering the travelers' sight
And tripping with its roots.

The moon, a slender silver horn,
Gleams on the rosy-baldricked morn,
And, milky white, the mists below
From frosty fields are rising slow.
manual_comment: The metrical terminology here is drawn from A Handbook of Modern English MMetre (Mayor, 1875).

metadata_frame: The following question is based on a passage in Beneath Blue Skies and Gray, a book of poems published in 1898 by Ingram Crockett, an American poet and journalist.
main_question: Write four lines of iambic trimeter that rhyme abcb, on the theme of "Autumn Days."
answer_strings:
O gentle days of Autumn!
O holy days of gray!
That with an inner rapture
In meditation stray.

The leaves begin to fall
and gild the fading bough,
the light grows thin and cold—
I scarce remember how.

When summer's light begins to wane
And leaves begin to turn,
The days of autumn draw me back
And memory returns.

Oh, hark! the silver horn is blown,
Its witchery is round me thrown;
I saw it touch the Morning's lips
Held in her rosy finger-tips,
manual_comment: The metrical terminology here is drawn from A Handbook of Modern English MMetre (Mayor, 1875).

metadata_frame: The following question is based on a passage in Beneath Blue Skies and Gray, a book of poems published in 1898 by Ingram Crockett, an American poet and journalist.
main_question: Write a quatrain of iambic pentameter, rhyming abba, that describes a sunset by apostrophizing God.
answer_strings:
Spirit Immortal surely this must be
A vision of Thy glory: on the hills
A beauty not of earth, the light that fills
The chambers of Thy presence eternally!

O God, you drape the west in crimson flame
And pull the daylight slowly through your hands,
Till darkness pools like water over lands
And night reclaims the world from which it came.

O Lord of Hosts, the light now burning in the west
Reminds us, that in Thy world a guest
We sojourn, and those tints of flame
Welcome us back to the world whence we came.

Yet I forget not, thro' keen winter days 
Your kindly arms outstretched did keep the herds,
And now within your pleasant rooms the birds
Watched o'er by you sing love's own tenderest lays;
manual_comment: The metrical terminology here is drawn from A Handbook of Modern English MMetre (Mayor, 1875).

metadata_frame: The best answer to this question is drawn from Sea and Bay; a Poem of new England, a narrative poem published in 1916 by Charles Wharton Stork, an American poet and translator.
main_question: Write a short passage of autobiographical blank verse describing how the speaker, as a child, preferred the sea to human companions.
answer_strings:
I made no friends; as soon as school was done 
I used to trudge off gravely by myself
To lord it in the kingdom of my choice;
A pebbly beach, walled in on every side

I was a silent child, and kept apart.
The others shouted games across the fields;
Their laughter struck me like a foreign mart.
I walked instead the path that led to shore.

That once in the stillness of a summer's noon, 
While I was seated in a rocky cave
By the sea-side, perusing, so it chanced,
The famous history of the errant knight

I was a quiet child, and strayed
   Toward wave and wind alone.
The others played on fields of grass,
   But I where sand was sown.
manual_comment: The distractors are either not blank verse, not on theme, or anachronistic in diction.
source_title: Sea and Bay; a Poem of new England

metadata_frame: The best answer to this question is drawn from Sea and Bay; a Poem of new England, a narrative poem published in 1916 by Charles Wharton Stork, an American poet and translator.
main_question: Write a short passage of blank verse lamenting the dehumanizing effects of modern science and industry.
answer_strings:
All this has science wrought. So much is now
Done by machinery, that the race of men
Are scarcely more than tools in the great mill
Of modern industry.

We measured out the world and found it wanting--
reduced the sky to pressure, light to waves,
and carved the living body into parts
that function, fail, and can be replaced.

And now we are parsed
into preferences, sorted
into the bins of our desires,
each hunger noted, amplified, and sold back
before we have finished feeling it.

The sea would slumber like an odalisque
With silver bosom and voluptuous limbs
Foam-pillowed there beneath the passionate stars.
source_title: Sea and Bay; a Poem of new England

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from How to Write Letters: A Manual of Correspondence, a practical manual published in 1875 by J. Willis Westlake, an American author.
main_question: Your name is Jane Faithful. Provide a letter of introduction for Mr. William Worthy.
answer_strings:
Green Valley, Pa., May 1, 1876.

DEAR SIR,
I have the honor of introducing to your acquaintance Mr. William Worthy, whom I commend to your kind attentions.
Jane Faithful.

May 1st, 1876

To Whom It May Concern,
It is with very great pleasure, and in full confidence of a favourable reception, that I take the liberty of addressing you on behalf of Mr. William Worthy, who has done me the honour of requesting a letter of introduction to present upon his arrival in your city.
Permit me to remain, with the highest esteem and regard,
Your most obedient servant,
Jane Faithful

Miss Jane Faithful of Green Valley, Pennsylvania, begs leave to introduce Mr. William Worthy, a gentleman of good character and steady habits, whom she trusts will be received with the same civility and confidence she herself would gladly extend him.

May 1, 1876
Dear Friend,
I am happy to introduce to you Mr. William Worthy, who carries this letter. Bill and I have known each other for some years now, and I can recommend him to you without hesitation — he is thoroughly reliable, good company, and just the sort of person one is always glad to know.
With warmest regards,
Jane Faithful
manual_comment: This is a sample letter from the book. Distractors are manual or language model, and have errors both of fact and of style.
source_title: How to Write Letters: A Manual of Correspondence

But the point which concerns us now is simply this: The forth-reaching, questioning soul can never be satisfied if it touches only a dead wall in the darkness, if its seeking meets with the reply, "You do not know, and you never can know, and you must not try to know." Ernest Renan, "nothing interests me, or appears worthy my attention." [missing sentence describing an inference or effect] But suppose Renan is mistaken.

Therefore the ungodly shall not stand in the judgment:

Nor sinners in the congregation of the righteous.

Think of the result of existence in the man or woman who has lived chiefly to gratify the physical appetites; think of its real emptiness, its real repulsiveness, when old age comes, and the senses are dulled, and the roses have faded, and the lamps at the banquet are smoking and expiring, and desire fails, and all that remains is the fierce, insatiable, ugly craving for delights which have fled for evermore; think of the bitter, burning vacancy of such an end,—and you must see that pleasure is not a good haven to seek in the voyage of life.
This implies that for some individuals, the loss of faith can lead to existential despair and a sense of futility.
