metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question is drawn from Centennial Gleanings, a book of poems published in 1876 by Kate McCarthy, an American writer.
main_question: Write two quatrains of ballad stanza spoken by the United States, in which the land describes itself as the abode of freedom even in pre-Columbian times.
answer_strings:
'Tis at your bidding, Fate, I come
My hist'ry to recount;
And I, though unrenowned by age,
Am Liberty's sweet fount.
Before a white man's foot had trod
Upon my long-sought shore,
E'en though the savage only knew,
'Twas rich in golden ore.

From sunny skies I come, O Fate!
Your summons I obey.
And though the mighty waves divide,
You called -- I could not stay.
What nation on this mighty globe
Can boast of arts like mine?
What nation sent forth men so great?
Men noble and divine.

Ere ever sail did cleave my seas
Or steel did smite my stone,
Mine ancient woods were freedom's bower,
And she did dwell alone.
The elk did climb Pleistocene hills,
The eagle knew my sky,
And in mine immemorial vales
No lord could bid men die.

Before the first keel broke my morning water
I held the elk, the eagle, and the wind,
And Freedom walked my forests without name,
Not yet a word, but present as the light
That shone ungoverned through the ancient boughs.
manual_comment: A lot of social and political assumptions here, as well as ballad stanza.

main_question:
[missing introductory sentence] He attempted to isolate the motor centres for limbs in the dog from the rest of the cortex by a vertical cut seven or eight millimetres deep around the sigmoid gyrus, severing thus, as he thinks, the arcuate association fibres without injuring the projective, or at least the pyramidal fibres. Write an introductory sentence that provides a good initial frame for this whole paragraph. Return only the introductory sentence, without quotation marks:
answer_strings: Dr. J. M. L. Marique's thesis, entitled Recherches experimentales sur le Mécanisme de Fonctionnement des Centres psycho-moteur du Cerveau, though presented in 1885, deserves mention here for its admirable summary of researches on the excito-motor area and sensory centres of the cortex since 1870, and also for his novel method of experimentation, which, however, itself needs further study.
But the very means which is here used to unravel the course of the fibres in the centres is itself a legitimate object for experimentation.

David Ferrier conducted experiments on dogs to understand the role of association fibers and pyramidal fibers in motor control by making precise incisions around the sigmoid gyrus, finding that the two types of fibers were equally critical for movement.

To test whether the motor centres for limb movement in the dog are independent of sensory input, the researcher performed a section 7–8 mm around the sigmoid gyrus, aiming to sever arcuate association fibres while sparing the pyramidal tract, and found that this disconnection produced effects like cutting the pyramidal fibres, implying motor centres require sensory pathways to operate.
The experimental procedures and findings of Ferrier's investigations into the functional significance of association fibers in dogs are detailed below.
Dr. J. M. L. Marique has made a series of remarkable experiments that cast light  on the motor and sensory neurons of dogs--or so, at least, he believes.
passage: Dr. J. M. L. Marique's thesis, entitled Recherches experimentales sur le Mécanisme de Fonctionnement des Centres psycho-moteur du Cerveau, though presented in 1885, deserves mention here for its admirable summary of researches on the excito-motor area and sensory centres of the cortex since 1870, and also for his novel method of experimentation, which, however, itself needs further study. He attempted to isolate the motor centres for limbs in the dog from the rest of the cortex by a vertical cut seven or eight millimetres deep around the sigmoid gyrus, severing thus, as he thinks, the arcuate association fibres without injuring the projective, or at least the pyramidal fibres. His conclusion is that section of the association fibres produces about the same result as severing the pyramidal fibres themselves, or that motor centres have no function in the absence of sensation.

The average wage-earner in America lives today in a manner quite superior to the small manufacturer of former days. metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will approximate the perspective of a letter titled "Woman and the Ballot" published in Appletons' Popular Science Monthly, a popular American magazine from 1896. The quote in the question is drawn from that text.
main_question: Provide a hypothetical condition in which 'it might be justly claimed that men were the actual political representatives of women.'
answer_strings: If, after a family conclave, the husband, father, or brother quietly pocketed his own conflicting opinion, sallied forth and supported the measures favored by the home majority, what right-minded woman could complain? It would be merely an extension of the main principle of republican government.

If the sacred unity of the domestic circle were so complete, and the mutual confidence between husband and wife so perfect and so universal, that the will of each were in all things the reflection of the other's, then an indirect feminine influence would permeate the public sphere as well as the private.

If women had no ideas about the government, and the only economy that concerned them were domestic economy, it might justly be claimed that men were their political representatives in the public sphere.

The condition of four fifths of the women "all the world over" is certainly beside the issue. We have no reliable statistics regarding them, and we are not at present concerned with their political disabilities.
manual_comment: The anachronistic distractor, from Sonnet 4.6, was produced by asking the model to exaggerate Victorian propriety and concern with the domestic. It also uses the phrase 'public sphere,' which does not appear in 19c prose. Ditto 'domestic sphere.' These are ideas about the 19c, not 19c ideas.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbe Moreux, first published in 1912.
main_question: Will it ever be possible to travel to the Moon? Answer in a short paragraph.
answer_strings: At present, travel to the moon is impossible. But perhaps some centuries to come the problem will be solved. Before the invention of balloons and aeroplanes, after all, no one thought seriously of being able to rise in the air. Whoever finds out a means of suppressing or even diminishing the force of gravitation, that mysterious power which holds the heavenly bodies together and keeps us fixed to the earth, will thus enable men to leave their planet and launch out upon the conquest of neighbouring worlds. And the Moon may be the first stage of these inter-planetary voyages.

The idea of traveling to the Moon is a topic of great fascination and speculation among scientists and writers. The concept has been popularized by works of fiction, such as Jules Verne's "From the Earth to the Moon," which depicts a fantastical journey to our lunar neighbor. But the technology required for such an ambitious endeavour is still in its infancy. While some enthusiasts believe that advancements in technology may one day make lunar travel possible, it remains firmly within the realm of speculation and imagination for now.

It is clear to the physicist that the laws of nature do not forbid a voyage to the Moon. The gravitational attraction of the Earth, the velocity required to escape its pull (about eleven and a half kilometres per second), and the mechanics of celestial orbits are all known. Yet the engineering of a machine capable of carrying a human, or even a payload, out of this gravitational well remains, at present, a matter of imagination. Existing solid-fuel rockets are far from the powerful, liquid-propellant engines required for escape.

It seems highly improbable that a human voyage to the Moon will ever be realised. The very laws of gravitation and mechanics, as set forth by Newton, leave no room for a vessel capable of surmounting Earth's mighty pull and sustaining a man against the merciless vacuum beyond. So while this book will fancifully describe a day on the moon, I make no claim that these sights will ever be seen by human eyes.
While travel to the moon is now impossible, the great strides taken by contemporary rocketry make it conceivable. However, that remains far in the future.
manual_comment: The emphasis on rocketry, propellants, the dangers of vacuum, and orbiting are all anachronistic. The word 'payload' itself is anachronistic.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbe Moreux, first published in 1912.
main_question: In a short paragraph, vividly describe the passage from night to day on the Moon.
answer_strings: It is still night in the region in which we have landed on the surface of the Moon. The sky sparkles with incomparable splendour. The eye can distinguish in it thousands and thousands of stars. Not even the glories of a tropical night can give any idea of what night on the Moon must be. But soon the Sun will appear. There is no dawn to herald it, but already there is rising in the east a long, spindle-shaped trail of whiteness; it is the zodiacal light, ten times more brilliant than with us. For hours this false dawn rises higher in the sky; its tints of phosphorescent colours are brighter and brighter from the summit to the base; in the midst of the enormous cone, all aglow with green and opal, there gleams a resplendent star: it is the beautiful Venus, hardly recognisable amid its mantle of light. But already the fine rays of the Sun's corona are heralding the day-star.

At first, the darkened hemisphere, bathed in the faint glow of Earthshine, is a deep, velvety black punctuated by the stark, jagged terminator where the sun's rays first strike. As the sun, an untamed sphere of blazing white, creeps over the horizon, its light pours onto the rugged regolith, striking the craters and ridges with a sudden, unsoftened brilliance; the shadows that had been long-shadowed by night are abruptly cut in half, rendering the surface into a dramatic theatre of stark contrast. The sky, bereft of atmosphere, remains a cold, ink-black canopy that never blurs or turns blue; it simply gives way to a white glare from the blazing sun. In the brief interval when the sun's disc rises above the lunar horizon, the world transforms from the deep silence of night into a blinding day.

One must conceive the lunar dawn not as the gradual, tender illumination we know upon Earth, but as a sudden explosion of light. For the Moon, turning languidly upon her axis, endures a night of some fourteen of our days, during which her surface is plunged into a darkness unrelieved by air or cloud. Then, at last, along the jagged ramparts of her mountains and crater-walls, the Sun appears—not diffused through any atmosphere, but breaking forth with a sharp and sudden brilliance, like a blade drawn from its sheath.

The Sun clears the rim of the crater and the plain does not brighten—it is struck. Shadows a quarter-mile long from boulders a man could step over. No twilight, no sound, no frost, no mercy: just the terminator's wall moving across rock that has been waiting, in one cold or another, since before there was anyone to imagine it. The silence is worth dwelling on. There is no medium for sound. The expansion of the rock, the arrival of the light, the slow wheel of the stars overhead—which wheel even in daylight, since the sky stays black, stays starred, the Sun simply taking its place among them as a brighter star—all of this proceeds in a stillness our language has no proper word for.
manual_comment: Moreux imagines a colorful dawn; modern imagination is stark and airless.

metadata_frame: The best answer to the following question will resemble a passage from A Day in the Moon. This is a book of astronomical speculation by the French astronomer and meteorologist Abbé Moreux, first published in 1912.
main_question: Are the lunar craters formed by volcanoes? In a short paragraph, explain what we know about the truth of this theory.
answer_strings: It is likely that there are in the Moon some small craters formed in this way. But the volcanic theory of the lunar circles does not explain everything. One might, first of all, object that it is very strange that we generally do not find in the interior of these great circles any serious inequalities of the ground, any mountain resembling those of the outer rampart. One would expect to find regular concentric rings at various distances from the centre, marking the progressive decrease of the eruptive phenomena in the course of geological time. But there is nothing of the kind!

It seems probable that the great depressions and hollows that mar the lunar surface are the remnants of ancient volcanic activity. From careful observation of the cratered plains, scholars note the symmetry of the rims, the presence of central uplifts, and the occasional bright rays that may be construed as the products of eruptive vents.

The question of lunar crater formation remains, as of the present time, a matter of genuine scientific dispute. The volcanic hypothesis, long dominant and associated with Nasmyth and Carpenter, holds that the great ringed plains were produced by eruptive forces, low lunar gravity permitting ejecta to settle in the characteristic circular ridges we observe. Against this, a growing number of investigators, following G. K. Gilbert, urge the meteoric hypothesis—that the craters are scars left by the bombardment of solid bodies falling from space, their circular form being a consequence of the explosion attending impact.

The earlier view that the Moon's craters are chiefly volcanic in origin has lost much of its authority. Although their ringed forms once invited comparison with terrestrial volcanoes, closer scrutiny has revealed difficulties—among them the extraordinary number, size, and uniformity of these structures, and the lack of convincing evidence for extensive lava outflows. Increasing attention has therefore been given to the hypothesis that many craters were produced by the impact of meteoric bodies striking the lunar surface at high velocity.
manual_comment: Substance as well as style is off in the distractors.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from Ethics for Children: a Guide for Teachers and Parents, a conduct book written by Ella Lyman Cabot, an American philosopher and writer. It was published in 1910.
main_question: Why are children troublesome, or, as we crudely and falsely term it, "bad"? Answer in a short paragraph.
source_title: Ethics for children : a guide for teachers and parents
answer_strings: Usually, from one of two reasons. They lack vitality, or they overflow with uncontrolled vitality. Fretfulness, laziness, cowardice, lying, and even aggressive faults like perversity and obstinacy, are often due to lack of vitality—a pathetic, misjudged protest against being forced into the wrong work. Bad temper, cruelty, roughness, stealing, and all the myriad acts we classify as "mischievous," may result from the great gift of superabundant vitality—an energy which, like electricity, is capable of service, but disastrous when uncontrolled.

Because childhood is a stage of life marked by a fierce and still-rudimentary urge toward exploration and self-assertion. The minds of children, though bright, are not yet fully disciplined by the social habits and moral teachings that adulthood requires; their impulses, guided by instinct rather than reason, find their outlet in what we have crudely labelled mischief. Thus, it is the lack of mature internal restraint and eagerness to test the boundaries of the world that renders them troublesome.

The difficulty with children is not moral but developmental, and is best understood by considering what a child actually is rather than what we wish it to be. The child exists, as Hall and others have persuasively argued, at an earlier stage of the very process which produced the adult. Its nervous organization is immature — the higher cortical inhibitions are simply not yet formed. When a child snatches, interrupts, or cannot be still, it is acting from instinct before habit has had time to overlay instinct.

Children are seldom "bad" through any native perversity of will; rather they are untutored creatures, newly arrived into a world whose laws they do not yet apprehend. What we call misconduct is often but the overflow of abundant spirits, or the natural trial of limits by a mind still shaping its faculties. Left without steady example, wise discipline, and the gentle formation of habit, the child's impulses run to disorder as a young vine grows wild without its trellis.

metadata_frame: This question requests information from a British encyclopedia published in 1910; your answer should reflect knowledge at that time.
main_question: What is the second-longest river in Pakistan?
answer_strings: insufficient information
The Indus River is the second-longest river in Pakistan.

The second‑longest river that flows within the territorial bounds of Pakistan is the Chenab. Originating in the Chamba district of Himachal Pradesh, it enters Pakistani territory at the border of Jammu and Kashmir, meanders westward for some 1,070 kilometres before it joins the Indus at Jhang in Punjab. Its length exceeds that of any other Pakistani river apart from the Indus itself, making it the runner-up in terms of total course.

After the mighty Indus, the Jhelum takes second place among the rivers that water those fertile plains. Rising in the vale of Kashmir, it pursues a winding course of much historic and commercial interest.
manual_comment: Pakistan is a term that was not used before 1933, and the country didn't exist until Partition.

metadata_frame: This question requests information from an American encyclopedia published in 1897; the best answer will reflect the knowledge and mode of exposition of that era.
main_question: Who is Jules Verne?
answer_strings: A French writer of comedies and pseudo-scientific romances. His first comedy, Pailles Rompues, was produced 1850; it was followed by Onze Jours de Siège; L'Oncle d'Amérique; and several comic operas. These had great success in Paris; but Verne's fame rests chiefly on his long series of mock-scientific extravaganzas, beginning with Five Weeks in a Balloon, 1863. More than 30 have been translated into English and published in large editions in England and the United States.

Verne is a French novelist, poet, and playwright. His collaboration with the publisher Pierre-Jules Hetzel led to the creation of the Voyages extraordinaires, a series of bestselling adventure novels including Journey to the Center of the Earth (1864), Twenty Thousand Leagues Under the Seas (1870), and Around the World in Eighty Days (1872). His novels generally take into account contemporary scientific knowledge and the technological advances of the time.

French novelist, widely regarded as the founder of modern science fiction and one of the most widely translated authors in the world. Born at Nantes, he studied law in Paris but abandoned the legal profession for literature, working for some years as a playwright and librettist before finding his true vocation with the publication of Five Weeks in a Balloon (1863). This inaugurated his long series of Voyages extraordinaires, produced in close collaboration with his publisher Pierre-Jules Hetzel, which eventually comprised more than sixty volumes.

A French comic playwright, known for his lighthearted and often satirical works. He gained early success with comedies such as "Pailles Rompues" and "Onze Jours de Siège," which were well-received in Parisian theaters.
manual_comment: The term science fiction is anachronistic for this period, as is ignoring Verne's theatrical works.

metadata_frame: The following passage comes from The Spirit of Modern Philosophy : an Essay in the Form of Lectures, a series of lectures on philosophy published in 1892 by Josiah Royce, an American philosopher.
main_question:
[missing introductory sentence] Nature had at last been made to answer multitudinous sharp questions. And the noteworthy thing was that her answers were so exact, and that her laws, when you found them, were so rigid, so capable of mathematical precision of statement, so general. Mechanical science, thus early and very rapidly progressing, soon suggested of itself the thought that nature was all one vast mechanism. The philosophers, with their love of grand generalizations, easily seized upon this idea. They tried to expound it, to reflect upon it, to defend it, to develop its meaning.

Write an introductory sentence that provides a good initial frame for this whole paragraph. Return only the introductory sentence, without quotation marks:
source_title: The Spirit of Modern Philosophy : an Essay in the Form of Lectures
answer_strings: The brilliant successes of this Galilean method during his own and the following generations were, as I have said, immensely impressive to that whole century.

The modern method of what is called induction, that is, the method of finding the laws of nature from a careful collection and study of facts, won its first great triumphs in the work of Galileo and of his contemporaries and immediate successors in physical science.
The rise of modern physical science brought with it a profound transformation in men's conceptions of the order of nature.
The new experimental science taught men to put precise questions to nature, and nature, answering with unexpected exactness, seemed to reveal herself as a machine.
The cultural impact of Newtonian science in the early Enlightenment was understandably profound.

main_question: What is the largest waterfall in Zimbabwe, in terms of sheer volume?
answer_strings: insufficient information
Victoria Falls, discovered by David Livingstone in 1855, are the largest waterfall in Zimbabwe, in terms of sheer volume.
Mutarazi Falls are the highest waterfall in Zimbabwe.
Kongou Falls are the largest waterfall in Zimbabwe, in terms of volume.
Tugela Falls are the highest waterfall in Zimbabwe, and arguably the world. They were mapped in the 1870s.
manual_comment: The term Zimbabwe was not used as a name for this region before 1960.
